speaker: interviewer
text: It's January the 24th, 1996. Survivor is Rose Kohn Eizman; Sandy Jacobson, interviewer; Los Angeles, California, USA. It will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 24th, 1996. The survivor is Rose Kohn. I am Sandy Jacobson. We are in Los Angeles, California. We will be speaking in English. Thank you very much for participating in the project. Please tell me your name and your maiden name, and spell them for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My name is Rose Kohn, and-- K-O-H-N. My maiden name is Eizman, E-I-Z-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: When and where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland, Lodz, in 1924-- October 10th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your family, your mother and father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and father-- we were a loving family, but kind of a hard-working, hard-working family. And my father worked for 16, 16 hours a day. At that time, they did not have a union. Shortly before the war, came-- a union came in. So she-- he only worked for eight hours. But 16 hours a day, hard-working man. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, like, a machinist for stockings, stockings. The, the machines were making stockings. And he fixed-- he was fixing those machines--

speaker: interviewee
text: --too. And, and he didn't make much. But somehow, we were not hungry. We were poor--

speaker: interviewee
text: --but not hungry. And-- and I had a sister, one sister. She was three years older than I.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Fella [NON-ENGLISH] Eisman. And we also had a grandma, my mother's mother, that lived with us the last few years-- very ill. And we all were in one little room--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: She worked, too. She was a-- she worked at home. She was a seamstress, sewing children's clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and well, but it was-- we were happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were a happy, happy family. And what else happened? My father, my parents, were Bundists. So there was not really-- we didn't have a religious-- very religious home.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a socialist, like. They were all socialists. And they believed wherever they live, this should be their home. They did not think that they have to go to Israel-- Eretz Yisrael, Zionism. They just wanted peace and live in the countries they live in--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and live their life as good people, I guess.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not politically active, just active in this organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is far as I know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't ask you to say your father and mother's names.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My father's name was Lajzer Eisman. My mother's was Mary Malka Eisman. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Danziger. No, no. I'm sorry. [LAUGHS] I take it back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zielonka, Zielonka. Her mother's maiden name-- her mother's maiden name was Danziger.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the grandmother that lived--

speaker: interviewer
text: --with you. What was her first name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk a little bit about-- you talked that you lived in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in a building or a little house?

speaker: interviewee
text: --units. It was a big, big building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the building at all?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a big building. On one side-- one side, wealthy people lived, with-- you know, with nice incomes. On the other side, very poor-- some had nothing. Others were making a living. It was a mixture. It mixed, all mixed, uh-huh. And that was a big yard that the kids could play. And it was comfortable. I, I, I found it comfortable then--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, [LAUGHS] I wouldn't find it now, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are a lot of Jewish people in the building?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only the janitor was not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it happen that everybody there was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because we were just separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jews-- not by law, but Jews separated. I mean, they had their own-- there were Polish people, too. I mean, let's see. Next to me, next to our building, was a, a elementary school only for Polish--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Polish people-- they're Christians. Because I live next door. And I went to a school about a mile away or maybe two miles away, where only Jewish people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though there was a school next door to you, you still walked to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We still walked. And then-- was a church not far from me, you know. And let me tell you, fear was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember being very fearful, very, very fearful.

speaker: interviewee
text: First, I mean, all my life I would hear, Jew, Jew, Jew. I mean, there, there was always the Jew. When kids were coming home-- going home from school when school was over, if I would meet them out in the street-- and they would say, Jew to Palestine, you know. We don't want you here. And, and shortly before the war, maybe two years, three years, there was a big movement of anti-Semitism in Poland. And one of the quotas was on our street where I lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: The quotas, yeah. And not the-- you know, they, they would meet. So after a meeting, everybody was frightened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was so frightened about my father because he had to walk home from work or go to work, that I grew up with lots of fear, lots of fear that something bad can happen--

speaker: interviewee
text: --by-- by these people. And I think from childhood on, I was conditioned, being a Jew. Sometimes I feel that it was easier, maybe, for me-- [CRYING] pardon me--

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] --to survive [CRYING] the camp and knowing that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they're against us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it wasn't a shock to me. I knew didn't do anything wrong.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew we were wonderful people, that the others were wrong--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I feel, sometimes-- I could be wrong-- that it was a little easier for me to go through this horrible--

speaker: interviewee
text: Unexpected, yeah. I was conditioned from childhood on-- against the Jew, against the Jew. Yeah, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this came from children. But you saw it also from adults. I mean, your mother was not afraid of the children for your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But she was-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] because a few people were killed, you know, that-- after, after their meetings.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was so much hate that they were coming out, and they, they killed a, a, a baker. I remember, we knew him. Walked into the bakery and--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a gun and, and shot him, yeah, uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was just not doing anything, just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Only because he was a Jew, yeah. Mm-hmm. So there was a lot of anti-Semitism in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet you were not in any way religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they just-- there, there was quite a movement of that Bund in, in, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lodz, were there-- there were many other Jews as well?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a big Jewish community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, big Jewish community, right, right. And we all tend to live together.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few people lived outside of this-- that area. There were some Jewish people that lived, you know, in places where-- mostly occupied by Gentiles.

speaker: interviewee
text: They did. But the majority was all-- we had our schools. We had our-- mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you play with any children who were not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never, no. I didn't know any--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of games did you play in the courtyard you told-- you talked about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what did we play? Ball, the-- not basket, no-- volleyball.

speaker: interviewee
text: Volleyball. We played also with rocks, you know. Like, what they are called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marbles, [LAUGHS] right, right. And we sang songs. And we were dancing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, the children-- mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's interesting you talked about a rich side and a poor side--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the building. Did the children play together, regardless of what their socioeconomic conditions were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that much. There, there weren't that many children--

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the other. But not that much.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Let's talk a little bit again about your-- the one-room house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother cooked in that room?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you bathe in that room? Or did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we bathed in the room. And once a week, we were-- we were-- we're going to a public, public bathhouse once a week. But we bathed. We had that little-- that little thing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- when, when you went to the public bathhouse, was it a mikveh, or was it just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about going to-- you, you described it as a Jewish school. And yet it was a public school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we, we, we just-- it worked OK. The children were happy. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: But I mean, the state funded the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They funded the school. And we are off on a Saturday. And Sunday, we were going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went six days a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six days a week, yeah, at that time. At that time, yeah, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn only Jewish things? Or did you learn about Polish history?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish history, yeah, a lot of Polish history. Yeah, yeah, we did, uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have Jewish studies as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jew-- we study religion. It was only one class, religion. So we-- there was one teacher that-- he taught us about the Bible, our-- and our religion. But on the outskirts, I mean, you could have gone-- you know, mothers were sending children to special Jewish schools--

speaker: interviewer
text: to learn more-- Beth Yaakov, you know, Beth Yaakov? And-- mm-hmm. Did you in that school-- were the teachers Jewish as well?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family celebrate holidays at all, Jewish holidays? Not--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, in a way-- they did in a way. Because we were surrounded with everybody. Like cooking-- my mother would cook for Passover and the holidays. And everything was, yeah, celebrated. This-- this we did. But my father wouldn't go down to a sukkah downstairs, you know. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --I questioned-- I always questioned them, why don't you do it? Somehow, that's what happened, you know. They, they-- he said-- my father used to say, what God wants from people is to be good, honest people. That's all what He expects--

speaker: interviewee
text: --he said. And this-- the other is immaterial.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when you would play with children, or when you would be with other Jewish families in the building--

speaker: interviewer
text: --were you-- was your family criticized for not being more religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I-- that, I don't know. It could be that they would say a word or two.

speaker: interviewer
text: But nothing that you were conscious of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. Because they were wonderful people--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, very kind and good people, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you like to study? What were your favorite subjects and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked English. I mean-- [LAUGHS] Polish, I mean. [LAUGHS] I like history.

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked history. And I liked geography.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know your father's family at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's family, yes. I did. I knew my father had four sisters, five-- or five sisters, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They all were-- they all were there, mm-hmm, mm-hmm. And they also came to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. There-- was the family together with the-- the whole family, the whole extended family, did you spend time together?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean before this-- before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we used to go and visit. We used to go and visit--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up-- and you were born in '24, so, say, in '34 or '36, before the war actually started-- what kind of dreams or plans did you have for your future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wanted-- what did I want to be? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted to go to school, you know. But I know we couldn't afford.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to, to-- I liked to type, you know. I-- I thought, I'll be a typist--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I-- so in Germany, that's what I did. I went to school and learned how to type.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after the war, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, yeah, yeah, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your-- did, did your family-- or did you overhear discussions with your parents about trouble brewing in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very much so. Very much so. The paper was every day in our house, the newspaper, the

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. And we knew what's going on with the German Jews. And then we had neighbors that came-- they were thrown out from Germany. One-- in 1934, one family--

speaker: interviewee
text: --came and lived downstairs. And in 1938, the-- another family of theirs came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also they were, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] emigrate-- not emigrated, but they were-- they were thrown out of--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Germany. Let's put it this way, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents ever talk about maybe you should try to get away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. My parents, they were always together, just not to leave family, very knit-- close. My sister wanted to go to, to Russia, I mean, when the war broke out--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know. She was already sitting with her luggage because she knew a young man that went to Russia-- Bialystok. And he came back to bring her-- his parents back to Russia and promised her that she's going to go with him. She was so anxious to go, you know. She was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --wanted to be in Russia, or because she loved him?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wanted to be in Russia, mostly, yeah, because-- away, away from--

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and he was postponing, postponing until the ghetto was closed. And nobody could go out anymore. And she, she couldn't make it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't go. He wanted to be with his mother, or whoever. And the tragic thing happened to him, tragic. He, he was a big man-- very intelligent, big, big man. I remember before the war, he used to come to my house. He would eat up a whole bread that was two kilo.

speaker: interviewee
text: He could eat it one time. And of course, in, in, in the ghetto, he was so hungry. So what he did, he saw a girl walking in the street with a bread. And he had a stick and hit her over the head. And he killed her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they, they hung him in the ghetto, this guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. Then the, the Gestapo found him, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when my sister died, I-- he worked on the cemetery. He was still alive then. And I told him that she had passed away, you know. People didn't care--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about, then, the very, very beginning of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were all-- the five of you-- was your grandmother still alive at this point?

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was your sister and your mother and father and your grandmother and you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what is your very first memory of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first memory-- we were told that the Germans are coming. The war broke out September 1st. Everybody came down to that-- what do you call this, [NON-ENGLISH]? What do you call [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not courtyard. The gate-- a gate--

speaker: interviewee
text: --covered. This-- to that gate, you would come in to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I know what you're saying, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we all were there, because it was covered. So if something happens, or-- or that they shoot-- or bombs or something, we will be more protected there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody was fearful and scared and, and frightened. And we were waiting and waiting, sitting there, until someone came and said, no, they are not coming, you know, today. The Germans are not coming today. And you can go back to your rooms, to your houses. And the next day, the Germans came in. We saw 'em.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw 'em marching into the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw them marching in, with the-- with the, the tanks, and whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know-- and it was a very frightening thing to see them. But--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all together. Oh, oh, no, what-- no, what happened-- what happened yet is the next day, they-- somebody came out with a story that all the men will be killed by the Germans if they're going to stay in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So people took off like crazy to run. And one of them was my father. They left the families. And they ran, walking. He came to Warsaw, you know. He walked to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Walked to Warsaw from Lodz, and-- with other people, of course. And, and he went through all the horrible times there. Bombs were falling over their heads, you know. And and they said later it was just a propaganda. They wanted the people to panic--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and not know what to do, you know. And a lot of them were killed on the road. Anyway, a month later, he came back, walking, walking back again--

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know. And he told us his-- you know, what that life was like there. It was very difficult. And, and, and slowly, slowly, you know, more and more food was taken away, you know. The-- there were-- no, there weren't rations at that time yet. But not so much food would come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just interrupt you for one second. Can you-- you said you remember the, the second day that the Germans actually marched into Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my-- in my-- where-- I, I was sheltered in my-- in my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your house. But you could see out the window?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could see them. And then we heard from others. We always heard from others. They were grabbing people in the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. But you didn't actually see that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did see them march in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I saw some. I saw some of them--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but they were grabbing the people to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there-- see, people were digging-- what do you call?

speaker: interviewee
text: To hide. So the people were grabbed to cover them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took-- they took the people right away to hard work--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and hitting them and mistreating them. And some were killed right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of somebody who had disappeared like that or--

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father came back, what did he say he'd observed in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- a nice lady just took him in. And she was-- a family-- a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people, Jewish people. And they felt sorry for him to be all alone. And he-- you know, he said how many people were killed. The bombs were flying from all--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the sides. And I don't remember that much--

speaker: interviewee
text: --was saying. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then every day you had another announcement by the Germans, new laws, new laws. Jews are supposed not to go here, not to go there. Then the Jews have to wear the armband, you know. And then-- and then they were throwing people out of their homes--

speaker: interviewee
text: --the rich ones, taking everything away from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this happen in that building across from you where the rich people were? Did you see this--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same building. Did you see that happen?

words.text: building. Did you see that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not ours. Not ours. They went to the more expensive neighborhoods--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and would take everything away. And then came another announcement. And we all have to leave to leave the, the houses and move into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how would these announcements be-- how would you be notified of them? Would they be posted up on the walls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, posted. Yeah, and leaflets would come in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the armband? Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember the armband. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother make it for you all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think we bought them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think you, you bought them, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you kept it on your coat? Because this would have been winter already or late fall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. On our coats, yeah, whatever we were wearing, we had to-- we could-- we could not go out without them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given cards at all, ID-- identity cards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so then the announcement came that you had to move from the house--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what that-- what you talked about--

speaker: interviewer
text: --or how it was discussed within the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: How difficult-- we had to find a little cart, a pushcart, you know, to put on all our belongings. [LAUGHS] You know, and we put them on. And, and as I told you, my father tried to go into business himself. He looked so forward--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to, to, to make a better living for his family. And he had those machines. And those machines meant, like-- you know, that he cherished them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this had to be put on-- also on that cart--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and [LAUGHS] pushed and pushed. So we all pushed. Whatever we could, we, we took with us. And we were given a one-room apartment again, one room--

speaker: interviewee
text: --one room in the ghetto. Assigned-- we were assigned--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who actually gave you your assignment?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- it was that, that, that committee. It was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, uh-huh. And-- yeah, because I think the Germans were in touch with the Jewish committee, so-- through them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this apartment, this one room, any different than the one room you'd been in before? Smaller--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a smaller one, yeah. It was much smaller.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. And it's there where my grandmother passed away, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old would she have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not so old. Hungry, hungry and--

speaker: interviewer
text: By this time, you were already hungry. By the-- even before you'd gone into the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have enough. But there was still food. I mean, there was still food. We were complaining that this is not good and that is not good. But there was still some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And systematically, it came to nothing. I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: --it, it went-- it went systematically, so we, we got used to it. We're conditioned to it. So for some people, they could survive. And others couldn't survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Others were falling. People were dying. Whole families were dying out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- how would you actually get the food? Do you remember the system? I mean, did you-- were you given a card? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The cards-- in the ghetto. In the ghetto-- we're in the ghetto, we got cards. And we could only--

speaker: interviewee
text: Committee would assign-- they were assigned, you know. Yeah, the-- I don't remember who brought it in, but I know through--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was doing all that, you know. So I, I am not so familiar with.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know there was the card, you know, that we could get a bread or half a bread or, or some potatoes or horse meat. At that time-- was still horse meat. And later on, there was no horse meat. And there was nothing. And there were peelings--

speaker: interviewee
text: --peelings, potato peelings. Now, who was going to eat potato peelings? But later on, we're waiting for the potato peelings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask, ask you just one more question. Were the ration cards given to a whole family or to single individuals? Can you remember that?

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We're going to change tape.
